title: Why You're More Comfortable Talking About Sex Than Money
body:
Why You're More Comfortable Talking About Sex Than Money

You're probably more comfortable talking about your last sexual encounter than you are about your last bank statement. Right?! You know it's true. You might blush, squirm, or even laugh nervously, but I bet you'd rather detail a questionable hookup from college than reveal your current credit score to a stranger. Or even to a friend. Or, let's be honest, sometimes even to yourself. How does that make you feel?

Here's the thing. We live in a society that simultaneously glorifies wealth and demonizes talking about it. We're bombarded with images of luxury, success, and financial freedom, yet the actual mechanics, the struggles, the fears, the shame around money - that's all kept in the shadows. We're taught to pursue it relentlessly, but never to truly look at it, speak about it, or understand our deep, often twisted, relationship with it. It's a bizarre paradox, isn't it? This isn't just about good manners or social etiquette. It's about something far deeper, far more primal, and frankly, far more damaging.

Money is just energy with a story attached to it. That's it. But oh, the stories we create. The narratives we inherit. The beliefs we cling to. They're dense, they're sticky, and they're often completely unconscious. We carry them around like invisible burdens, dictating our choices, influencing our emotions, and shaping our entire lives. And because we're so conditioned to keep these stories secret, they fester. They grow. They become more powerful than they ever should be. Think about it. When was the last time you had a truly open, honest, vulnerable conversation about money with someone you trust? Not just "Can you lend me twenty bucks?" but a real, heart-to-heart about your financial fears, your hopes, your past mistakes, your deepest desires?

The Shame Spiral: Why Money Feels So Personal

Honestly, the biggest reason you'd rather talk about anything else is shame. Pure, unadulterated shame. Brené Brown has done incredible work on shame and vulnerability, and her findings apply directly to our money lives. Shame thrives on secrecy, silence, and judgment. And when it comes to money, we're swimming in it. If you have debt, you feel shame. If you don't have enough, you feel shame. If you have too much, you might feel guilt, which is just shame's sneaky cousin. If you make a mistake, if you overspend, if you get ripped off, if you can't provide, if you feel trapped - shame. It's an insidious cycle.

Money is inextricably linked to our sense of self-worth, our identity, and our perceived value in the world. It's not just numbers in a bank account; it's a mirror reflecting back all our deepest insecurities and fears. If you believe, consciously or unconsciously, that your financial situation is a direct reflection of your intelligence, your capability, your morality, or your worthiness of love, then of course you're going to hide it. Of course you're going to avoid talking about it. Who wants to expose their perceived failures to the world? Who wants to admit they're not 'good enough' in the one area society so loudly proclaims defines success?

This shame isn't always about what you've done. Often, it's about what you haven't done, or what you feel you can't do. It's the shame of aspiration not met, potential not realized, security not achieved. It's the whispered "I should be further along" or "Why can't I figure this out?" It's the fear of being judged as irresponsible, incompetent, or foolish. And so, we build walls. We create elaborate facades. We become masters of deflection and avoidance. But these walls, while seemingly protecting us, also keep us isolated, alone, and stuck.

Notice what your body does when you think about your debt. Does your stomach clench? Do your shoulders rise? Does your breath get shallow? That's your body reacting to the emotional weight of it all. It's not just a cognitive problem; it's a somatic experience. You feel that? Your body holds the score, as they say. And until you start to acknowledge that physical reaction, until you bring some compassionate awareness to it, you're just going to keep running from it.

The Invisible Hand of Our Financial Inheritance: Money Scripts

You didn't invent your money problems from scratch. You inherited them. Brad Klontz, a leading financial psychologist, talks extensively about "money scripts" - unconscious beliefs about money that we learn in childhood and that dictate our financial behaviors as adults. These scripts are passed down through generations, often unspoken, but deeply felt. They're the underlying narratives that shape our entire financial worldview.

Did your parents constantly fight about money? You might have a script that says "money causes conflict" and unconsciously sabotage your own financial success to avoid arguments. Were you told "money doesn't grow on trees"? You might struggle with scarcity, always feeling like there's not enough, even when there is. Were you taught that "rich people are greedy"? You might feel guilty about making money, pushing it away as soon as it comes in. Were you told "money is evil" or "spiritual people don't care about money"? You might struggle to align your spiritual values with your financial goals, creating an internal tug-of-war that keeps you stuck.

These money scripts aren't just thoughts; they're deeply ingrained patterns. They're the lens through which you see every financial decision, every opportunity, every challenge. And because they're largely unconscious, you don't even realize they're running the show. You just think "that's just how I am with money." But it's not. It's a learned behavior, a survival mechanism developed in childhood to make sense of your family's financial reality. It Didn't Start with You (paid link) by Mark Wolynn is a fantastic resource for understanding how these inherited family traumas and patterns influence everything, including our money stories.

Think about the financial messages you received growing up. Were they explicit? "We can't afford that." "Save for a rainy day." "Don't trust banks." Or were they implicit? The tension in the air when bills arrived. The hushed conversations about debt. The sudden splurges followed by periods of extreme frugality. All of these contribute to your money script. And until you identify these scripts, until you bring them into the light of conscious awareness, they'll continue to control your financial destiny. You can't change what you don't acknowledge. It's like trying to work through a dark room without turning on the light. You're going to bump into things, trip, and stay lost.

The Power of Vulnerability: Reclaiming Your Financial Story

So, what do we do about it? We start talking. We start being vulnerable. We challenge the shame. We expose the scripts. It's not easy. It will feel incredibly uncomfortable at first. But remember Brené Brown's work: vulnerability is not weakness; it's courage. It's the birthplace of connection and meaning. When you bravely share your financial truth, you create space for others to do the same. You dismantle the shame, one honest conversation at a time.

This doesn't mean airing all your dirty laundry to everyone you meet. It means finding safe spaces, safe people, where you can begin to practice financial vulnerability. Maybe it's a trusted friend, a partner, a therapist, or a financial coach. Someone who can listen without judgment, someone who can offer empathy and understanding. Someone who can hold space for your fear, your anger, your regret, your confusion.

Tara Brach, a renowned meditation teacher, talks about radical acceptance. It's about bringing a kind, open, and non-judgmental awareness to whatever is present. This applies beautifully to your money story. Can you radically accept where you are financially right now, without judgment, without shame, without wishing it were different? Can you simply observe the thoughts, feelings, and sensations that arise when you think about your money, without immediately trying to fix them or push them away?

This isn't passive resignation. It's active awareness. It's the first step towards true change. Because once you can accept what is, you can then compassionately inquire into what needs to change. You can't fix a problem you refuse to look at. And you can't heal a wound you pretend doesn't exist.

Money as a Spiritual Practice: Beyond Scarcity and Greed

What if your relationship with money is the most honest relationship you have? Think about it. Money doesn't lie. It reflects your spending habits, your beliefs about worth, your fears, your desires, your priorities. It shows you exactly where you're putting your energy, whether consciously or unconsciously. It's a powerful feedback mechanism, a mirror reflecting your inner world. If you're constantly feeling scarcity, where in your life are you feeling scarce? If you're constantly chasing more, what void are you trying to fill?

Geneen Roth, in her book Lost and Found (paid link) (and her earlier work on food and emotional eating, which has direct parallels to money), talks about how our relationship with food is never just about food. It's about control, love, fear, comfort, self-worth. The same is absolutely true for money. Your relationship with money is never just about money. It's about your deepest beliefs about yourself, about life, about security, about love, about freedom. It's a spiritual practice in disguise.

When you approach money from this perspective, everything shifts. It's no longer just a cold, hard, transactional thing. It becomes a path to self-discovery, a tool for growth, a teacher. You start to see how your money patterns are linked to your emotional patterns, your relational patterns, your spiritual patterns. You begin to understand that true wealth isn't just about accumulating more; it's about aligning your financial life with your deepest values and living in integrity. It's about freedom - not just financial freedom, but emotional and spiritual freedom from the grip of fear and shame.

This doesn't mean ignoring practical financial advice. Far from it. It means integrating the practical with the spiritual. It means understanding the psychology of money, as explored in books like The Psychology of Money (paid link) by Morgan Housel, but also recognizing that the emotional and spiritual dimensions are equally, if not more, powerful. You can have all the financial knowledge in the world, but if your unconscious money scripts and shame are running the show, you'll constantly sabotage your efforts. You'll find ways to stay stuck, even when you know better.

Breaking the Silence: Practical Steps Towards Healing

So, how do you actually start to heal this relationship? How do you break the silence and reclaim your financial power? It starts small, with conscious, consistent effort.

  1. Acknowledge the Shame: The first step is always awareness. Recognize when shame comes up around money. Don't judge it; just notice it. "Ah, there's that familiar shame again." That simple act of noticing creates a tiny bit of space between you and the emotion.
  2. Identify Your Money Scripts: Spend some time journaling. What were the messages you received about money growing up? What did your parents or caregivers believe about money? What did they do with money? Write it all down. See if you can identify recurring themes or beliefs that might be impacting you today.
  3. Find a Safe Confidante: Choose one person - a partner, a trusted friend, a therapist, a coach - and have an honest conversation. Start small. "I've been realizing I have a lot of shame around money, and I want to talk about it more openly." See how it feels. The act of speaking it aloud, even to one person, is incredibly powerful.
  4. Practice Financial Mindfulness: Instead of avoiding your bank statements or bills, approach them with mindfulness. Take a deep breath. Notice any physical sensations or emotions that arise. Just observe. Don't react. This is radical acceptance in action.
  5. Educate Yourself (Compassionately): Learn about personal finance, but do it from a place of curiosity, not self-judgment. Read books, listen to podcasts, take a course. The more you understand how money works, the less mysterious and intimidating it becomes.
  6. Set Intentions, Not Just Goals: Instead of just setting a goal like "save $10,000," set an intention around how you want to *feel* about money. "I intend to feel secure and abundant." "I intend to use money to create a life of purpose." This shifts the focus from external accumulation to internal alignment.
  7. Celebrate Small Wins: Every time you look at your budget, every time you pay a bill on time, every time you have an honest conversation about money - celebrate it. Acknowledge your courage and your effort. Reinforce the positive steps you're taking.

This isn't a quick fix. It's a journey. It's a relationship you're building, just like any other important relationship in your life. It requires patience, compassion, and a willingness to get uncomfortable. But the payoff is immense: true financial freedom, not just in your bank account, but in your heart and mind.

FAQ

Why is it so hard to talk about money?

It's hard because money is deeply intertwined with our sense of self-worth, identity, and security. We often carry unconscious shame, fear, and inherited beliefs (money scripts) about money that make us feel vulnerable and exposed when discussing it. Society also often treats money as a taboo subject, reinforcing the idea that it should be kept secret.

What are "money scripts" and how do they affect me?

Money scripts are unconscious, often generational, beliefs about money that we develop in childhood. They dictate our financial behaviors and attitudes as adults. For example, if you grew up hearing "money is evil," you might unconsciously push wealth away. If you heard "there's never enough," you might constantly feel scarcity. These scripts run in the background, influencing your choices without your conscious awareness, often leading to self-sabotage or chronic financial stress.

How can I start to heal my relationship with money?

Start by acknowledging any shame or fear you feel around money. Practice financial mindfulness by observing your reactions to money-related thoughts or tasks without judgment. Identify your inherited money scripts through journaling. Find one trusted person (a partner, friend, therapist) to begin having honest conversations about money. Educate yourself about personal finance from a place of curiosity, and set intentions for how you want to *feel* about money, not just what you want to accumulate. Celebrate small steps towards greater awareness and financial health.

Look. This isn't about becoming a financial wizard overnight. It's about becoming conscious. It's about healing. It's about reclaiming a vital part of your life that you've probably been avoiding. So, today, just for today, I challenge you to take one small, uncomfortable step. Look at your bank balance, not with dread, but with a tiny spark of curiosity. Or tell one trusted person, "I'm trying to get more honest about my money." Just one small thing. Do it. You've got this.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Discover why discussing sex feels easier than money. Explore the societal and psychological reasons behind this common discomfort. Understand your financial com
author: Kalesh
